user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
1) those buyers who are waiting for lower prices at or below the previous liquidity zone, will be unsure if the price will every fall back, so they go up to the resistance and start to buy.
with that, you see increasing buying volume at the resistance, and it gets broken
comment.raw_body:
1) those buyers who are waiting for lower prices at or below the previous liquidity zone, will be unsure if the price will every fall back, so they go up to the resistance and start to buy.
with that, you see increasing buying volume at the resistance, and it gets broken

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
2) those buyers won't get interestend, they don't want to pay that amount of price for the asset. at the resistance levels, the buyers will eventually run out, who filled the sell orders of the sellers, so there will be no more buyer at those levels, so the sellers will be forced to start to place their orders lower...
resistance rejects, market turns downward

comment.body:
+1) if the hesitation, back and forth goes on too long, dealers will come in, hunt stoplosses to juice up liquidity.
simply bcs market cannot stagnate.
if market stagnates, volatility naturally declines.
and vol cannot be zero. 
market cannot stop

comment.body:
the key is this: 
- resistances are tested by sellers,
- supports are tested by buyers.

bcs sellers want to sell at higher prices,
buyers want to buy at lower prices

comment.body:
a simple RSI is a good invention.
sometimes it can help. not a holy grail, but it is usefull for technicals.

it is simply the (green candles)/(red candles) over the past period you set.
(turning into an oscillator, for the easier read).

so if the selling volume increases, the ratio will decrease, as the denominator increases relative to teh numerator.
and if the buying activity increases, the ratio will increase, as the numerator increases faster relative to the denominator.
math
